subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Definition of a Fluid
desc: A fluid is a substance that deforms continuously under the action of shear stress, no matter how small. Solids resist shear by deforming to a fixed strain, while fluids flow and maintain a constant strain rate.
formula: Shear stress tau = F/A; for fluids, tau proportional to strain rate du/dy
trap: 🚨 Students often confuse fluids with solids: solids deform to a finite strain; fluids never stop deforming under shear. Also, gases and liquids are both fluids, but only liquids have a free surface.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: No-Slip Condition
desc: A fluid in direct contact with a solid surface sticks to the surface and has zero velocity relative to it. This viscous effect creates a velocity gradient near the wall and is responsible for boundary layer formation.
formula: V_fluid_at_wall = V_wall (relative velocity = 0)
trap: 🚨 Many students think fluid can slip at a solid boundary. The no-slip condition is fundamental for real fluids; ignoring it leads to incorrect drag and heat transfer predictions.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Internal vs External Flow
desc: Internal flow occurs when fluid is completely bounded by solid surfaces (e.g., pipe flow). External flow involves an unbounded fluid moving over a surface (e.g., flow over an airfoil). Internal flows are dominated by viscosity throughout; external flows have thin boundary layers.
trap: 🚨 Mistaking open-channel flow (with a free surface) as internal flow; open-channel flow is a special category, not purely internal.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Compressible vs Incompressible Flow
desc: Incompressible flow assumes constant density; it is valid for liquids and gases at Mach numbers less than 0.3. Compressible flows involve significant density changes, important at high speeds (Ma > 0.3).
formula: Ma = V/c (Mach number); Incompressible approximation: Ma < 0.3
trap: 🚨 Believing that all gas flows are compressible. Actually, gas flows can be treated as incompressible if speed is low enough (Ma < 0.3) with less than 5% density change.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Laminar, Turbulent, and Transitional Flow
desc: Laminar flow is smooth and orderly with fluid moving in layers. Turbulent flow is chaotic with velocity fluctuations. Transitional flow alternates between both regimes. Reynolds number determines the regime.
formula: Re = (rho V D)/mu = (V D)/nu
trap: 🚨 Assuming laminar flow always occurs at low velocities and turbulent at high velocities; the actual regime also depends on fluid properties (viscosity) and characteristic length (pipe diameter).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Steady vs Unsteady Flow
desc: Steady flow means no change at a point with time (∂/∂t = 0). Unsteady (or transient) flow changes with time. Periodic flow oscillates about a steady mean.
formula: Steady: ∂/∂t = 0 for all properties; Unsteady: ∂/∂t ≠ 0
trap: 🚨 Confusing 'steady' with 'uniform'. Steady refers to time independence; uniform refers to spatial independence. A flow can be steady but non-uniform (e.g., fully developed pipe flow).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: System and Control Volume
desc: A system (or control mass) is a fixed quantity of matter; no mass crosses its boundary. A control volume (open system) is a fixed region in space; mass and energy can cross its boundary.
trap: 🚨 Students often misapply conservation laws by not clearly identifying whether they are analyzing a control mass or control volume. For devices like nozzles, use control volume; for piston-cylinder, use closed system.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Dimensional Homogeneity
desc: Every term in an equation must have the same units. If you add two quantities with different units, you have made an error. Checking dimensions is a valuable tool for spotting mistakes.
trap: 🚨 Adding or equating quantities with inconsistent units (e.g., energy in kJ and energy per unit mass in kJ/kg). Always use unity conversion ratios to ensure dimensional consistency.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Mass, Weight, and Unit Systems
desc: Mass (kg, lbm) is a measure of matter; weight (N, lbf) is the gravitational force acting on a mass: W = mg. In SI, 1 N = 1 kg·m/s². In English, 1 lbf = 32.174 lbm·ft/s². The pound-mass and pound-force are often confused.
formula: W = m g; 1 N = 1 kg·m/s²; 1 lbf = 32.174 lbm·ft/s²
trap: 🚨 Using lbm and lbf interchangeably as 'pounds' leads to serious errors. On earth, 1 lbm weighs 1 lbf only because g = 32.174 ft/s² numerically matches the conversion factor. This is a common trap in USCS calculations.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Accuracy, Precision, and Significant Digits
desc: Accuracy is how close a measurement is to the true value; precision is the repeatability or fineness of measurement. Significant digits indicate the reliability of a reported value. Calculations should not report more significant digits than the least precise input.
trap: 🚨 Reporting too many digits from a calculator implies false precision. Always round results to the appropriate number of significant digits based on input data. For example, 3.75 L × 0.845 kg/L should be 3.17 kg, not 3.16875 kg.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Intensive vs Extensive Properties
desc: Intensive properties are independent of the mass of a system (e.g., temperature, pressure, density). Extensive properties depend on the system's size or extent (e.g., total mass, total volume). Dividing a system into two equal parts leaves intensive properties unchanged while extensive properties are halved.
trap: 🚨 Students often mistake mass as intensive because they think of density; but mass is extensive, while density is intensive.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Density, Specific Gravity, Specific Weight
desc: Density is mass per unit volume. Specific gravity is the ratio of a substance's density to the density of water at 4°C. Specific weight is weight per unit volume.
formula: rho = m/V; SG = rho / rho_H2O; gamma = rho * g
trap: 🚨 Confusing specific gravity with density; SG is dimensionless. Using SG as density in calculations without multiplying by rho_water leads to errors.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Ideal Gas Equation of State
desc: Relates pressure, density, and temperature for an ideal gas: P = rho R T, where R is the gas constant. For a fixed mass, P1 V1 / T1 = P2 V2 / T2. The continuum model applies when the mean free path is much smaller than the characteristic length.
formula: P = rho R T; Pv = RT; PV = mRT
trap: 🚨 Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in ideal gas law. Always convert to absolute temperature (K or R).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Vapor Pressure and Cavitation
desc: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by vapor in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature. When local pressure drops below vapor pressure, vapor bubbles form; their violent collapse causes cavitation, leading to noise, vibration, and erosion.
formula: P_v = P_sat(T)
trap: 🚨 Ignoring cavitation risk in pumps, turbines, and propellers; assuming liquids are always incompressible without considering pressure drops below vapor pressure.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Energy Forms and Specific Heats
desc: Total energy includes internal, kinetic, potential, and flow energy. For flowing fluids, enthalpy h = u + P/rho represents microscopic energy plus flow work. Specific heats at constant volume and pressure relate changes in internal energy and enthalpy.
formula: ke = V^2/2; pe = gz; h = u + P/rho; du = c_v dT; dh = c_p dT
trap: 🚀 For liquids, c_p ≈ c_v, but enthalpy change includes pressure term: Δh = c ΔT + ΔP/rho.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Coefficient of Compressibility (Bulk Modulus)
desc: Measures a fluid's resistance to compression. It is the change in pressure per fractional change in volume (or density) at constant temperature. For an ideal gas, κ = P.
formula: kappa = -v (∂P/∂v)_T = rho (∂P/∂rho)_T ≈ ΔP / (Δrho/rho)
trap: 🚨 Thinking liquids are perfectly incompressible; they have finite compressibility, though small. Water hammer is a consequence of compressibility.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Coefficient of Volume Expansion
desc: Measures the change in volume (or density) with temperature at constant pressure. For an ideal gas, β = 1/T.
formula: beta = (1/v)(∂v/∂T)_P = -(1/rho)(∂rho/∂T)_P ≈ (Δv/v)/ΔT
trap: 🚨 Confusing β with compressibility; β is for temperature change, κ for pressure change. Both affect density.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Viscosity: Dynamic and Kinematic
desc: Viscosity measures a fluid's resistance to deformation. In Newtonian fluids, shear stress is proportional to velocity gradient: τ = μ du/dy. Dynamic viscosity μ; kinematic viscosity ν = μ/ρ.
formula: tau = mu du/dy; nu = mu/rho
trap: 🚨 Forgetting that viscosity of liquids decreases with temperature, while gases increase. Confusing dynamic with kinematic viscosity in Reynolds number calculations.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Surface Tension and Capillary Effect
desc: Surface tension σ_s is the force per unit length acting at a liquid-gas or liquid-liquid interface, causing droplets to be spherical. Capillary effect is the rise or fall of liquid in a small tube due to surface tension.
formula: DeltaP_droplet = 2 sigma_s / R; DeltaP_bubble = 4 sigma_s / R; h = (2 sigma_s cos phi) / (rho g R)
trap: 🚨 Using wrong factor for bubble vs droplet; bubble has two surfaces so ΔP = 4σ_s/R. Contact angle determines whether rise or fall.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Density and Mass of Air in Room
problem: Determine the density, specific gravity, and mass of the air in a room whose dimensions are 4 m × 5 m × 6 m at 100 kPa and 25°C.
solution_steps: Use ideal gas law: ρ = P/(RT).
R for air = 0.287 kPa·m³/kg·K.
Convert T = 25°C + 273 = 298 K.
Specific gravity = ρ/ρ_water = 1.17/1000 = 0.00117.
Volume = 4×5×6 = 120 m³.
formula_used: ρ = P/(RT); m = ρV; SG = ρ/ρ_H2O
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin in ideal gas law; using R incorrectly (must use correct R for air).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Minimum Pressure to Avoid Cavitation
problem: In a water distribution system, the temperature of water is observed to be as high as 30°C. Determine the minimum pressure allowed in the system to avoid cavitation.
solution_steps: Cavitation occurs when local pressure drops below vapor pressure.
From Table 2-2, vapor pressure of water at 30°C is 4.25 kPa.
Therefore, minimum pressure must be ≥ 4.25 kPa.
trap: 🚨 Using saturation pressure at a different temperature; not accounting for dissolved gases that can lower cavitation threshold.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Density Change with Temperature and Pressure
problem: Consider water initially at 20°C and 1 atm. Determine the final density of water (a) if it is heated to 50°C at constant pressure of 1 atm, and (b) if it is compressed to 100-atm pressure at constant temperature of 20°C. Take the isothermal compressibility of water to be α = 4.80 × 10⁻⁵ atm⁻¹.
formula_used: Δρ ≈ -ρ β ΔT (constant P); Δρ ≈ ρ α ΔP (constant T)
trap: 🚨 Using wrong sign; density decreases with heating, increases with compression. Assuming β and α constant over large ranges may cause error.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Viscosity Measurement with Viscometer
problem: The viscosity of a fluid is to be measured by a viscometer constructed of two 40-cm-long concentric cylinders. The outer diameter of the inner cylinder is 12 cm, and the gap between the two cylinders is 0.15 cm. The inner cylinder is rotated at 300 rpm, and the torque is measured to be 1.8 N·m. Determine the viscosity of the fluid.
solution_steps: Gap thickness ℓ = 0.15 cm = 0.0015 m.
Radius R = 6 cm = 0.06 m.
Length L = 40 cm = 0.4 m.
Angular velocity ω = 300 rpm = 300×2π/60 = 31.416 rad/s (or ṅ = 5 rev/s).
Use formula μ = T ℓ / (4π² R³ ṅ L) with ṅ in rev/s.
formula_used: μ = T ℓ / (4π² R³ ṅ L) (ṅ = rpm/60)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s; using diameter instead of radius; neglecting end effects; assuming linear velocity profile is valid when gap/radius is small.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Capillary Rise in Glass Tube
problem: A 0.6-mm-diameter glass tube is inserted into water at 20°C in a cup. Determine the capillary rise of water in the tube.
solution_steps: Diameter = 0.6 mm → radius R = 0.3 mm = 0.0003 m.
Surface tension σ_s = 0.073 N/m (from Table 2-3).
Contact angle φ = 0° for water-glass.
Density of water ρ = 1000 kg/m³.
Capillary rise h = (2 σ_s cos φ)/(ρ g R).
formula_used: h = (2 σ_s cos φ)/(ρ g R)
trap: 🚨 Using diameter instead of radius in formula; forgetting to convert units consistently; assuming contact angle 0° when it may be different due to contamination.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pressure Definition and Units
desc: Pressure is defined as a normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area. It is a scalar quantity, and at a point in a fluid, it has the same magnitude in all directions. Common units include pascal (Pa), bar, atm, psi, etc.
formula: P = F/A (force per unit area). 1 Pa = 1 N/m^2, 1 bar = 100 kPa, 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, 1 psi = 6.89476 kPa.
trap: 🚨 Students often confuse absolute and gage pressure, forgetting that gage pressure is relative to atmospheric pressure.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Absolute, Gage, and Vacuum Pressure
desc: Absolute pressure is measured relative to absolute vacuum. Gage pressure is the difference between absolute pressure and local atmospheric pressure. Vacuum pressure is the pressure below atmospheric, equal to atmospheric minus absolute.
formula: P_gage = P_abs - P_atm (for pressures above atm); P_vac = P_atm - P_abs (for pressures below atm).
trap: 🚨 A common mistake is using gage pressure in ideal gas law or other equations that require absolute pressure.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pressure at a Point (Pascal's Law)
desc: Pressure at any point in a fluid at rest is the same in all directions. This is proven by considering a small wedge-shaped fluid element and applying force balance; as the element shrinks to a point, pressure becomes isotropic.
formula: P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = P (at a point)
trap: 🚨 Students may think pressure is a vector; it is scalar with magnitude independent of direction.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pressure Variation with Depth
desc: In a static fluid under gravity, pressure increases linearly with depth due to the weight of overlying fluid. For constant density, the pressure difference between two points separated vertically by Δz is ΔP = ρg Δz.
formula: dP/dz = -ρg (if z upward); P_2 - P_1 = -ρg(z_2 - z_1). For depth h from free surface: P = P_atm + ρgh.
trap: 🚨 Students may forget that pressure increases with depth, not decreases, and may apply the formula incorrectly for negative z.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Manometer Pressure Measurement
desc: A manometer uses a column of fluid to measure pressure differences. The pressure at a point is determined by starting at a known pressure and adding/subtracting ρgh terms as you move through fluid columns.
formula: ΔP = ρ_fluid g h (for simple U-tube). For stacked fluids: P_1 + Σ ρ_i g h_i (downward) = P_2.
trap: 🚨 Students often neglect the density of the gas or the correct sign when moving through different fluids.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Barometer
desc: A barometer measures atmospheric pressure by balancing the weight of a liquid column (usually mercury) against the atmosphere. At standard conditions, 760 mm Hg corresponds to 101.325 kPa.
formula: P_atm = ρ_mercury g h
trap: 🚨 The height of the column is independent of tube diameter; students might think a wider tube gives a different reading.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surface
desc: The resultant hydrostatic force on a plane surface submerged in a fluid equals the pressure at the centroid times the area. Its line of action (center of pressure) is below the centroid due to increasing pressure with depth.
formula: F_R = P_C * A = (P_0 + ρ g h_C) A; y_P = y_C + I_xx,C / (y_C A) (if P_0 = 0).
trap: 🚨 Students may assume the resultant force acts at the centroid, but it's actually at the center of pressure which is deeper.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrostatic Force on Curved Surface
desc: For a curved surface, the horizontal component equals the force on the vertical projection; the vertical component equals the weight of the fluid above (or below) the surface plus the force on the horizontal projection.
formula: F_H = force on vertical projection; F_V = weight of fluid block + force on horizontal projection; F_R = √(F_H^2 + F_V^2).
trap: 🚨 For curved surfaces above liquid, the weight of fluid is subtracted; students often add incorrectly.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Buoyancy and Archimedes' Principle
desc: A body immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. This force acts through the centroid of the displaced volume.
formula: F_B = ρ_f g V_displaced
trap: 🚨 Buoyant force does not depend on the weight of the body or its depth (if fully submerged). Students may think it changes with depth.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Stability of Immersed and Floating Bodies
desc: For immersed bodies, stability depends on relative positions of center of gravity (G) and center of buoyancy (B). For floating bodies, stability is determined by the metacentric height (GM > 0 stable).
formula: Metacentric height GM = (I_o / V_sub) - GB, where I_o is area moment of inertia of waterplane.
trap: 🚨 Students may think a floating body is always stable if G is below B; but for ships, G can be above B as long as metacenter is above G.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Fluids in Linear Acceleration
desc: When a fluid container accelerates linearly, the fluid behaves as a rigid body with no relative motion. The free surface becomes a plane inclined at an angle θ = arctan(a_x / (g + a_z)).
formula: tan θ = a_x / (g + a_z); P = P_0 - ρ a_x x - ρ (g + a_z) z (with appropriate sign).
trap: 🚨 The vertical acceleration a_z adds to gravity; students might forget to include it when the tank is accelerating upward or downward.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Fluids in Rotating Vessel
desc: When a cylindrical container rotates about its axis, the free surface forms a paraboloid of revolution. The pressure increases radially outward due to centrifugal force.
trap: 🚨 The free surface is parabolic, not linear. Students may incorrectly assume a linear slope like in linear acceleration.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Absolute Pressure in Vacuum Chamber
problem: A vacuum gage connected to a chamber reads 5.8 psi at a location where the atmospheric pressure is 14.5 psi. Determine the absolute pressure in the chamber.
solution_steps: Step 1: Recognize that the gage reads vacuum pressure, so absolute pressure = atmospheric pressure - vacuum pressure.
Step 2: Substitute values: P_abs = 14.5 psi - 5.8 psi = 8.7 psi.
trap: 🚨 Students may mistakenly add the vacuum pressure instead of subtracting.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pressure Measurement with Manometer
problem: A manometer is used to measure the pressure in a tank. The fluid used has a specific gravity of 0.85, and the manometer column height is 55 cm. If the local atmospheric pressure is 96 kPa, determine the absolute pressure within the tank.
solution_steps: Step 1: Calculate density of manometer fluid: ρ = SG × ρ_water = 0.85 × 1000 kg/m³ = 850 kg/m³.
Step 2: Apply hydrostatic equation: P = P_atm + ρgh.
Step 3: Substitute: P = 96 kPa + (850 kg/m³)(9.81 m/s²)(0.55 m) = 96 kPa + 4.6 kPa = 100.6 kPa.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert specific gravity to density or using incorrect units.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Multifluid Manometer Pressure
problem: The water in a tank is pressurized by air, and the pressure is measured by a multifluid manometer as shown in Fig. P3–9. Determine the gage pressure of air in the tank if h1 = 0.2 m, h2 = 0.3 m, and h3 = 0.46 m. Take the densities of water, oil, and mercury to be 1000 kg/m³, 850 kg/m³, and 13,600 kg/m³, respectively.
solution_steps: Step 1: Start at the air-water interface and move to the open end, adding ρgh for downward moves and subtracting for upward moves.
Step 2: Equation: P_air + ρ_water g h1 - ρ_oil g h2 - ρ_mercury g h3 = P_atm.
Step 3: Solve for P_air - P_atm = ρ_mercury g h3 + ρ_oil g h2 - ρ_water g h1.
formula_used: P_air + Σ ρ_i g h_i (downward) - Σ ρ_j g h_j (upward) = P_atm
trap: 🚨 Sign errors when moving between different fluids; forgetting that pressure increases downward.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Barometric Pressure Calculation
problem: Determine the atmospheric pressure at a location where the barometric reading is 740 mm Hg and the gravitational acceleration is g = 9.81 m/s². Assume the temperature of mercury to be 10°C, at which its density is 13,570 kg/m³.
solution_steps: Step 1: Use the barometric formula: P_atm = ρ_mercury g h.
Step 2: Convert height to meters: h = 0.74 m.
Step 3: Substitute: P_atm = (13570 kg/m³)(9.81 m/s²)(0.74 m) = 98500 Pa = 98.5 kPa.
trap: 🚨 Using density at 0°C instead of the given temperature, or forgetting to convert mm to m.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Piston Weight Effect on Cylinder Pressure
problem: The piston of a vertical piston–cylinder device containing a gas has a mass of 60 kg and a cross-sectional area of 0.04 m². The local atmospheric pressure is 0.97 bar, and the gravitational acceleration is 9.81 m/s². (a) Determine the pressure inside the cylinder. (b) If some heat is transferred to the gas and its volume is doubled, do you expect the pressure inside the cylinder to change?
solution_steps: Step 1: Draw free-body diagram of piston. Forces: atmospheric pressure downward, weight downward, gas pressure upward.
Step 2: Force balance: P_gas A = P_atm A + mg.
Step 3: Solve for P_gas: P_gas = P_atm + mg/A.
Step 5: Volume change does not affect the force balance, so pressure remains same.
final_answer: 1.12 bar; pressure unchanged with volume change
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert units (e.g., using N/m² to bar incorrectly) or assuming pressure changes with volume like ideal gas without considering free piston.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrostatic Pressure in Solar Pond with Variable Density
problem: Solar ponds have a salt gradient zone where density varies as ρ = ρ_0 √[1 + tan²(π/4 * z/H)]. For H = 4 m, ρ_0 = 1040 kg/m³, and surface zone thickness 0.8 m, calculate the gage pressure at the bottom of the gradient zone.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute pressure at top of gradient zone: P_1 = ρ_0 g h_surface = (1040)(9.81)(0.8) = 8.16 kPa.
Step 2: Integrate dP = ρ g dz over the gradient zone: P_2 = P_1 + ∫_0^H ρ(z) g dz.
Step 3: Given ρ(z) = ρ_0 √(1 + tan²(πz/(4H))) = ρ_0 sec(πz/(4H)).
Step 4: Perform integration: ∫_0^H ρ_0 sec(πz/(4H)) dz = (4H/π) ρ_0 ln|sec(πz/(4H)) + tan(πz/(4H))| evaluated.
Step 5: At z=H, argument = tan(π/4)=1, sec=√2, so integral yields (4H/π) ρ_0 ln(√2+1).
formula_used: P = P_1 + ∫ ρ(z) g dz; ρ(z) = ρ_0 sec(πz/(4H))
trap: 🚨 Mistaking the density variation as linear or forgetting to integrate properly; using average density yields error.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrostatic Force on Submerged Car Door
problem: A heavy car plunges into a lake and lands at the bottom. The door is 1.2 m high and 1 m wide, and the top edge of the door is 8 m below the free surface. Determine the hydrostatic force on the door and the location of the pressure center. Discuss if the driver can open the door.
solution_steps: Step 1: Average pressure at centroid: h_C = 8 + 1.2/2 = 8.6 m.
Step 3: Force F_R = P_ave × A = 84.4 × (1×1.2) = 101.3 kN.
Step 5: Depth h_P = y_P sin90° = 8.61 m. The moment about hinges is huge (~50 kN·m), impossible for a person to open.
formula_used: F_R = ρ g h_C A; y_P = y_C + I_xx,C/(y_C A)
final_answer: 101.3 kN; pressure center at 8.61 m depth
trap: 🚨 Using the centroid depth incorrectly (e.g., using full depth instead of centroid depth) or forgetting that pressure acts on both sides initially.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Gravity-Controlled Cylindrical Gate
problem: A long solid cylinder of radius 0.8 m hinged at point A is used as an automatic gate. When the water level reaches 5 m, the gate opens by turning about the hinge at point A. Determine (a) the hydrostatic force acting on the cylinder and its line of action when the gate opens and (b) the weight of the cylinder per m length of the cylinder.
solution_steps: Step 1: Horizontal force on vertical projection: F_H = ρ g h_C A = (1000)(9.81)(4.2 + 0.8/2)(0.8×1) = 36.1 kN.
Step 2: Vertical force on horizontal projection: F_y = ρ g h_bottom A = (1000)(9.81)(5)(0.8×1) = 39.2 kN.
Step 3: Weight of fluid block (quarter circle): W = ρ g (R² - πR²/4) = (1000)(9.81)(0.64 - 0.5027) = 1.3 kN.
Step 4: Net vertical component F_V = F_y - W = 39.2 - 1.3 = 37.9 kN.
Step 5: Resultant F_R = √(36.1² + 37.9²) = 52.3 kN; direction θ = arctan(37.9/36.1) = 46.4°.
Step 6: Moment equilibrium about hinge: W_cyl R sinθ = F_R R sinθ → W_cyl = F_R sinθ = 52.3 sin46.4° = 37.9 kN.
formula_used: F_H = ρ g h_C A; F_V = F_y - W; F_R = √(F_H² + F_V²); ΣM_A = 0
final_answer: F_R = 52.3 kN at 46.4° from horizontal; W_cyl = 37.9 kN per m length
trap: 🚨 Forgetting that the weight of the fluid above the curved surface is subtracted, or misidentifying the direction of vertical component.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrometer Specific Gravity Measurement
problem: A hydrometer floats in a vertical position. The top part extends above the liquid surface, and divisions allow reading specific gravity directly. (a) Obtain a relation for specific gravity as a function of distance z from the mark corresponding to pure water. (b) Determine the mass of lead to be poured into a 1-cm-diameter, 20-cm-long hydrometer if it is to float halfway (10 cm mark) in pure water.
solution_steps: Step 1: In pure water, buoyant force equals weight: ρ_w g A z_0 = W.
Step 2: In another fluid, ρ_f g A (z_0 + z) = W.
Step 3: Equate: ρ_f (z_0 + z) = ρ_w z_0 → SG = ρ_f/ρ_w = z_0/(z_0 + z).
Step 4: For part (b): radius = 0.5 cm = 0.005 m, submerged height = 0.1 m, volume = π(0.005)²×0.1 = 7.854×10⁻⁶ m³.
formula_used: SG = z_0/(z_0 + z); F_B = ρ_w g V_sub
trap: 🚨 Assuming the hydrometer's own weight is negligible, or using diameter instead of radius.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Weight Loss in Seawater
problem: A crane lowers a rectangular 0.4 m × 0.4 m × 3 m concrete block (density 2300 kg/m³) into the sea (density 1025 kg/m³). Determine the tension in the rope when (a) suspended in air and (b) completely immersed in water.
solution_steps: Step 1: Volume of block V = 0.4×0.4×3 = 0.48 m³.
Step 2: Weight in air W = ρ_concrete g V = 2300×9.81×0.48 = 10,830 N ≈ 10.8 kN.
Step 3: Buoyant force F_B = ρ_sea g V = 1025×9.81×0.48 = 4825 N ≈ 4.8 kN.
Step 4: Tension in water T = W - F_B = 10.8 - 4.8 = 6.0 kN.
formula_used: W = ρ g V; F_B = ρ_f g V; T = W - F_B
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert density to consistent units or neglecting buoyancy in air.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Water Tank Overflow during Acceleration
problem: An 80-cm-high fish tank of cross section 2 m × 0.6 m is initially filled with water. The truck accelerates from 0 to 90 km/h in 10 s. Determine the allowable initial water height to avoid spilling, and recommend tank orientation.
solution_steps: Step 1: Acceleration a = ΔV/Δt = (90/3.6)/10 = 2.5 m/s².
Step 2: Slope of free surface tanθ = a_x/(g + a_z) = 2.5/9.81 = 0.255 → θ = 14.3°.
Step 3: For long side parallel (length 2 m), rise at back = (L/2) tanθ = 1×0.255 = 0.255 m = 25.5 cm.
Step 4: For short side parallel (length 0.6 m), rise = (0.3)×0.255 = 0.0765 m = 7.65 cm.
Step 5: To avoid spill, initial water height should be ≤ tank height minus rise. Short side orientation gives smaller rise, so preferred.
final_answer: Short side parallel to motion; allowable water height = 80 - 7.65 = 72.35 cm (or less)
trap: 🚨 Using the full length instead of half-length for rise, or forgetting that vertical acceleration affects slope.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Liquid Rise in Rotating Cylinder
problem: A 20-cm-diameter, 60-cm-high vertical cylindrical container is partially filled with 50-cm-high liquid (ρ = 850 kg/m³). Determine the rotational speed at which the liquid will start spilling from the edges.
solution_steps: Step 1: Original volume V = πR² h_0 = π(0.1)²×0.5 = 0.015708 m³.
Step 2: Paraboloid free surface equation: z_s = h_0 + (ω²/(4g))(R² - 2r²).
Step 3: At edge (r=R), z_s(R) = h_0 + (ω² R²)/(4g). Set equal to tank height 0.6 m.
Step 5: Convert to rpm: n = ω×60/(2π) = 19.81×60/(2π) ≈ 189 rpm.
trap: 🚨 Using diameter instead of radius, or forgetting that the free surface is parabolic and using linear rise approximation.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Lagrangian vs Eulerian Descriptions
desc: Lagrangian description follows individual fluid particles (like tracking a billiard ball). Eulerian description defines field variables at fixed points in space (control volume). Lagrangian is system-based; Eulerian is control volume-based.
formula: Velocity field: V = V(x,y,z,t); Material derivative: D/Dt = ∂/∂t + (V·∇)
trap: 🚨 Confusing Lagrangian with Eulerian: In Lagrangian, you follow particles; in Eulerian, you stay at a point. Steady flow in Eulerian frame does not mean fluid particles are unaccelerated.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Material Derivative (Substantial Derivative)
desc: The material derivative D/Dt represents the rate of change following a fluid particle. It consists of local (unsteady) part ∂/∂t and advective (convective) part (V·∇). Applied to velocity, it gives acceleration.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting the advective term in steady flows leads to zero acceleration incorrectly. Fluid particles can accelerate even in steady flow (e.g., nozzle).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines
desc: Streamlines are curves tangent to instantaneous velocity vectors. Pathlines are actual paths of fluid particles. Streaklines are loci of particles that passed through a fixed point. In steady flow, all three coincide.
formula: Streamline: dx/u = dy/v = dz/w; Pathline: x = ∫ V dt; Streakline: x = ∫ V dt from tinject to tpresent
trap: 🚨 Assuming streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines are always the same; they differ in unsteady flow. Streaklines integrate history and can be misleading (e.g., smoke in vortex street).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Flow Visualization Techniques
desc: Experimental techniques include dye/smoke (streaklines), hydrogen bubbles (timelines), particle image velocimetry (PIV), schlieren, and shadowgraph (density-sensitive). Computational methods use vector and contour plots.
trap: 🚨 Misinterpreting a streakline as an instantaneous streamline, especially in unsteady flows (e.g., vortex street). Shadowgraph bright/dark regions may distort shape.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Rates of Motion and Deformation
desc: Fluid elements undergo translation (velocity), rotation (angular velocity), linear strain (stretching), and shear strain (angular deformation). Linear strain rates: ε_xx = ∂u/∂x, etc. Shear strain rate: ε_xy = ½(∂u/∂y + ∂v/∂x).
formula: Rotation: ω = ½(∇×V); Linear strain rate: ε_xx = ∂u/∂x; Shear strain rate: ε_xy = ½(∂u/∂y+∂v/∂x)
trap: 🚨 Confusing rate of rotation with vorticity; vorticity is twice the rotation. Also, volumetric strain rate is sum of linear strain rates (∇·V).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Vorticity and Rotationality
desc: Vorticity ζ = ∇ × V, twice the angular velocity. Rotational flow has nonzero vorticity; irrotational flow has zero vorticity. Example: solid-body rotation is rotational; line vortex is irrotational (except at origin).
formula: ζ = ∇ × V; For 2D xy-plane: ζ = (∂v/∂x - ∂u/∂y) k
trap: 🚨 Assuming circular streamlines always imply rotational flow. A line vortex (uu = K/r) has circular streamlines but is irrotational. Fluid particles do not spin.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)
desc: RTT relates the time rate of change of an extensive property B for a system to that for a control volume. It accounts for local changes and net flux across control surface. For fixed CV: dBsys/dt = d/dt∫CV ρb dV + ∫CS ρb V·n dA.
formula: dBsys/dt = d/dt∫CV ρb dV + ∫CS ρb Vr·n dA (general); for fixed CV, Vr = V
trap: 🚨 Using absolute velocity when control volume is moving; must use relative velocity Vr = V - VCS. For steady flow, local term is zero but system property may still change.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Stagnation Point in 2D Flow
problem: A steady, incompressible, two-dimensional velocity field is given by V = (u, v) = (0.5 + 0.8x)i + (1.5 - 0.8y)j. Determine if there are any stagnation points and where.
solution_steps: Thus stagnation point at (x, y) = (-0.625, 1.875).
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to set both components to zero; assuming stagnation point exists without checking.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Acceleration Through Nozzle
problem: Water flows through a nozzle with inlet diameter 0.420 in, outlet diameter 0.182 in, length 3.90 in, volume flow rate 0.841 gal/min. Estimate the acceleration of a fluid particle along the centerline.
solution_steps: Convert units: inlet speed u_in = (4V̇)/(πD_in²) = 4×0.00187/(π×0.0350²) = 1.95 ft/s.
Outlet speed u_out = 4×0.00187/(π×0.01517²) = 10.4 ft/s.
Average speed = (1.95+10.4)/2 = 6.175 ft/s.
Residence time Δt = Δx / u_avg = 0.325/6.175 = 0.0526 s.
Acceleration ≈ (u_out - u_in)/Δt = (10.4-1.95)/0.0526 ≈ 160 ft/s².
Alternatively using advective term: a_x = (u_out² - u_in²)/(2Δx) = (10.4²-1.95²)/(2×0.325) = 160 ft/s².
formula_used: a_x = ∂u/∂t + u∂u/∂x ≈ (u_out² - u_in²)/(2Δx) for steady flow
trap: 🚨 Thinking steady flow means zero acceleration; forgetting advective acceleration.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Material Acceleration at a Point
problem: For the velocity field V = (0.5+0.8x)i + (1.5-0.8y)j, calculate the material acceleration at (x=2 m, y=3 m).
solution_steps: Compute partial derivatives: ∂u/∂x = 0.8, ∂u/∂y = 0, ∂v/∂x = 0, ∂v/∂y = -0.8.
Acceleration vector: a = 1.68i + 0.72j m/s².
trap: 🚨 Including local acceleration ∂V/∂t (zero) but forgetting to compute advective terms correctly.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Streamlines in 2D Velocity Field
problem: For V = (0.5+0.8x)i + (1.5-0.8y)j, find an equation for streamlines and plot in right half.
solution_steps: Separate variables: dy/(1.5-0.8y) = dx/(0.5+0.8x).
Simplify: ln|1.5-0.8y| = -ln|0.5+0.8x| + constant.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting constant of integration; misapplying separation of variables.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Kinematic Properties in 2D Flow
problem: For V = (0.5+0.8x)i + (1.5-0.8y)j, calculate rate of translation, rotation, linear strain rates, shear strain rate, and volumetric strain rate.
solution_steps: Rate of translation = V = (0.5+0.8x)i + (1.5-0.8y)j.
Rotation: ω_z = ½(∂v/∂x - ∂u/∂y) = ½(0 - 0) = 0.
Linear strain rates: ε_xx = ∂u/∂x = 0.8 s⁻¹; ε_yy = ∂v/∂y = -0.8 s⁻¹; ε_zz = 0.
Shear strain rate: ε_xy = ½(∂u/∂y+∂v/∂x) = ½(0+0)=0.
Volumetric strain rate = ε_xx+ε_yy+ε_zz = 0.8 - 0.8 + 0 = 0 (incompressible).
final_answer: Translation: V; Rotation: 0; ε_xx=0.8 s⁻¹; ε_yy=-0.8 s⁻¹; ε_xy=0; Volumetric=0
trap: 🚨 Mixing rotation with vorticity; vorticity = 2ω, so here ζ = 0, not 2*0? Actually ω=0, so flow is irrotational.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Rotationality of a 2D Flow
problem: For V = (u, v) = (x²)i + (-2xy - 1)j, determine if the flow is rotational or irrotational.
solution_steps: Compute vorticity ζ_z = ∂v/∂x - ∂u/∂y.
ζ_z = -2y - 0 = -2y, which is not zero (except at y=0).
Hence the flow is rotational.
trap: 🚨 Assuming circular streamlines are always rotational; here streamlines are not circular but still rotational due to velocity gradients.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Vorticity of a Line Sink
problem: A line sink has velocity field ur = (V̇/L)/(2πr), uθ = 0. Determine the vorticity.
solution_steps: In cylindrical coordinates, for 2D flow (uθ=0, ur=K/r with K = V̇/(2πL)),
vorticity ζ_z = (1/r) ∂(r uθ)/∂r - (1/r) ∂ur/∂θ.
Since uθ=0 and ur independent of θ, both terms are zero.
Thus ζ_z = 0 everywhere except at r=0 (singularity).
trap: 🚨 Thinking radial flow must be rotational; line sink is irrotational because velocity varies as 1/r, producing zero curl.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)
desc: The principle that mass cannot be created or destroyed. For a control volume, the net mass flow rate into the volume equals the rate of change of mass within the volume.
formula: dm_CV/dt = Σ ṁ_in - Σ ṁ_out ; d/dt ∫_CV ρ dV + ∫_CS ρ (V·n) dA = 0
trap: 🚨 Students may forget to account for unsteady terms or use volume flow rate when density varies.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Mass Flow Rate and Volume Flow Rate
desc: Mass flow rate (ṁ) is the amount of mass crossing a cross-section per unit time. Volume flow rate (V̇) is the volume crossing per unit time. For incompressible flow, ρ is constant and ṁ = ρ V̇.
formula: ṁ = ∫_Ac ρ V_n dAc = ρ V_avg A_c ; V̇ = V_avg A_c
trap: 🚨 Using average velocity without accounting for velocity profile can lead to errors in kinetic energy calculations.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Steady-Flow Mass Balance
desc: For steady flow, the mass within the control volume does not change, so the total mass flow rate entering equals the total mass flow rate leaving.
formula: Σ ṁ_in = Σ ṁ_out ; for single stream: ρ1 V1 A1 = ρ2 V2 A2
trap: 🚨 Assuming steady flow without verifying that properties do not change with time at any point.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Mechanical Energy and Efficiency
desc: Mechanical energy is the form that can be directly converted to work (kinetic, potential, flow energy). Pump efficiency = (mechanical energy increase of fluid) / (shaft power input). Turbine efficiency = (shaft power output) / (mechanical energy decrease of fluid).
formula: e_mech = P/ρ + V^2/2 + gz ; η_pump = (Ė_mech,fluid) / Ẇ_shaft,in ; η_turbine = Ẇ_shaft,out / |Ė_mech,fluid|
trap: 🚌 Confusing motor efficiency with pump efficiency; overall efficiency is product.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Bernoulli Equation
desc: For steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure, kinetic, and potential energies per unit mass is constant.
formula: P/ρ + V^2/2 + gz = constant ; P1/ρ + V1^2/2 + gz1 = P2/ρ + V2^2/2 + gz2
trap: 🚨 Applying Bernoulli across a pump, turbine, or in regions with significant friction, compressibility, or unsteady effects.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Static, Dynamic, and Stagnation Pressures
desc: Static pressure is the thermodynamic pressure. Dynamic pressure is ρV^2/2, representing the pressure rise if fluid is brought to rest isentropically. Stagnation pressure is the sum of static and dynamic pressures.
formula: P_stag = P + ρV^2/2 ; V = √[2(P_stag - P)/ρ]
trap: 🚨 Using gage pressure incorrectly when absolute pressure is needed, or neglecting compressibility at high Mach numbers.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydraulic Grade Line and Energy Grade Line
desc: HGL = P/ρg + z ; EGL = P/ρg + V^2/2g + z. In frictionless flow, EGL is horizontal; HGL slopes downward where velocity increases. The difference is the velocity head.
formula: HGL = P/(ρg) + z ; EGL = P/(ρg) + V^2/(2g) + z
trap: 🚨 Mistaking HGL for total head; HGL can be below the pipe centerline indicating negative gage pressure.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)
desc: For steady flow, the net rate of energy transfer by heat and work equals the difference between outgoing and incoming energy flows with mass.
formula: Q̇_net,in + Ẇ_shaft,net,in = Σ_out ṁ (h + V^2/2 + gz) - Σ_in ṁ (h + V^2/2 + gz)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to include flow work (h = u + Pv) or neglecting kinetic/potential energy changes when significant.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Mechanical Energy Balance with Head Loss
desc: For steady incompressible flow, the mechanical energy equation includes pump head, turbine head, and irreversible head loss due to friction.
formula: P1/(ρg) + α1 V1^2/(2g) + z1 + h_pump,u = P2/(ρg) + α2 V2^2/(2g) + z2 + h_turbine,e + h_L
trap: 🚌 Using Bernoulli when head loss is significant; neglecting kinetic energy correction factor α in laminar flow.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Kinetic Energy Correction Factor α
desc: Correction factor used to account for non-uniform velocity profile when computing kinetic energy from average velocity. α = (1/A) ∫ (V/V_avg)^3 dA.
formula: α = 2 for laminar pipe flow; α ≈ 1.05 for turbulent flow.
trap: 🚨 Assuming α = 1 in laminar flow leads to underestimation of kinetic energy.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Garden Hose Nozzle Flow
problem: A garden hose attached with a nozzle is used to fill a 10-gal bucket. The inner diameter of the hose is 2 cm, and it reduces to 0.8 cm at the nozzle exit. If it takes 50 s to fill the bucket with water, determine (a) the volume and mass flow rates of water through the hose, and (b) the average velocity of water at the nozzle exit.
solution_steps: Step 1: Volume flow rate = (bucket volume) / (time) = (10 gal × 3.7854 L/gal) / 50 s = 0.757 L/s.
Step 2: Mass flow rate = ρ V̇ = (1 kg/L)(0.757 L/s) = 0.757 kg/s.
Step 3: Nozzle exit area = π (0.4 cm)^2 = 0.5027 cm² = 5.027×10⁻⁵ m².
Step 4: Average velocity = V̇ / A = (0.757×10⁻³ m³/s) / (5.027×10⁻⁵ m²) = 15.1 m/s.
formula_used: V̇ = V/t ; ṁ = ρ V̇ ; V = V̇ / A
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert gallons to liters or using diameter instead of radius.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Draining a Water Tank (Unsteady Mass Balance)
problem: A 4-ft-high, 3-ft-diameter cylindrical water tank whose top is open to the atmosphere is initially filled with water. Now the discharge plug near the bottom of the tank is pulled out, and a water jet whose diameter is 0.5 in streams out. The average velocity of the jet is given by V = √(2gh), where h is the height of water in the tank measured from the center of the hole. Determine how long it will take for the water level in the tank to drop to 2 ft from the bottom.
solution_steps: Step 1: Mass balance: -d(m_CV)/dt = ṁ_out → -ρ A_tank dh/dt = ρ A_jet √(2gh).
Step 2: Separate variables: dt = -(A_tank / A_jet) dh / √(2gh).
Step 3: Integrate from h0 = 4 ft to h2 = 2 ft: t = (A_tank / A_jet) √(2/g) (√h0 - √h2).
Step 4: Area ratio = (D_tank/D_jet)^2 = (36 in / 0.5 in)^2 = 5184.
trap: 🚨 Using diameter instead of radius in area ratio; forgetting that velocity depends on instantaneous h.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydraulic Turbine-Generator Performance
problem: The water in a large lake is to be used to generate electricity by the installation of a hydraulic turbine–generator at a location where the depth of the water is 50 m. Water is to be supplied at a rate of 5000 kg/s. If the electric power generated is measured to be 1862 kW and the generator efficiency is 95 percent, determine (a) the overall efficiency of the turbine–generator, (b) the mechanical efficiency of the turbine, and (c) the shaft power supplied by the turbine to the generator.
solution_steps: Step 1: Mechanical energy change per unit mass = gz = (9.81)(50) = 490.5 J/kg = 0.4905 kJ/kg.
Step 2: Total mechanical energy input = ṁ × Δe_mech = 5000 × 0.4905 = 2452.5 kW.
Step 3: Overall efficiency = Ẇ_elect / Ė_mech,in = 1862 / 2452.5 = 0.759 ≈ 0.76.
Step 4: Turbine efficiency = η_overall / η_generator = 0.76 / 0.95 = 0.80.
Step 5: Shaft power = η_turbine × Ė_mech,in = 0.80 × 2452.5 = 1962 kW.
formula_used: η_turbine-gen = η_turbine η_generator ; η_turbine = Ẇ_shaft,out / (ṁ g Δz)
trap: 🚨 Using gage pressure instead of head; confusing generator efficiency with turbine efficiency.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Spraying Water from a Hose (Bernoulli)
problem: Water is flowing from a hose attached to a water main at 400 kPa gage. A child places his thumb to cover most of the hose outlet, causing a thin jet of high-speed water to emerge. If the hose is held upward, what is the maximum height that the jet could achieve?
solution_steps: Step 1: Use Bernoulli between hose outlet (point 1, where V1≈0, z1=0, P1 = 400 kPa gage) and top of jet (point 2, V2=0, P2=0 gage, z2 = h).
formula_used: P/ρg + V^2/(2g) + z = constant → h = P_gage/(ρg)
trap: 🚨 Using absolute pressure instead of gage, or neglecting that velocity inside hose is not zero if hose is long.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Torricelli's Law (Tank Discharge)
problem: A large tank open to the atmosphere is filled with water to a height of 5 m from the outlet tap. A tap near the bottom of the tank is now opened, and water flows out from the smooth and rounded outlet. Determine the water velocity at the outlet.
solution_steps: Step 1: Apply Bernoulli between free surface (point 1: P1=Patm, V1≈0, z1=5 m) and outlet (point 2: P2=Patm, z2=0).
trap: 🚨 Using sharp-edged orifice without accounting for contraction, or applying when tank is not large enough to neglect velocity at surface.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Siphoning Gasoline (Minimum Time)
problem: A siphon is used to withdraw gasoline from a tank. Point 2 is 0.75 m below point 1, and point 3 is 2 m above point 1. The siphon diameter is 4 mm, and frictional losses are neglected. Determine (a) the minimum time to withdraw 4 L of gasoline and (b) the pressure at point 3. Gasoline density = 750 kg/m³, Patm = 101.3 kPa.
solution_steps: Step 1: Apply Bernoulli between 1 (free surface, V1=0, P1=Patm, z1=0.75 m) and 2 (outlet, P2=Patm, z2=0): V2 = √(2g×0.75) = 3.84 m/s.
Step 3: Time = 4 L / 0.0483 L/s = 82.8 s.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert diameter to radius, or ignoring that pressure at point 3 can drop below vapor pressure (cavitation).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pitot Tube Velocity Measurement
problem: A piezometer and a Pitot tube are tapped into a horizontal water pipe. The water column heights: h1 = 3 cm, h2 = 7 cm, h3 = 12 cm. Determine the velocity at the center of the pipe.
solution_steps: Step 1: Static pressure at point 1: P1 = ρg(h1 + h2).
Step 2: Stagnation pressure at point 2: P2 = ρg(h1 + h2 + h3).
Step 3: Apply Bernoulli between 1 and 2 (V2=0): P1/ρg + V1²/(2g) = P2/ρg → V1 = √(2g h3).
trap: 🚨 Using the total height difference incorrectly, or not recognizing that h3 is the dynamic head.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hurricane Ocean Swell Estimate
problem: A hurricane has atmospheric pressure 22.0 in Hg at the eye and 30.0 in Hg 200 mi away. Estimate the ocean swell at (a) the eye and (b) point 2 where wind velocity is 155 mph. Sea water density = 64 lbm/ft³, mercury density = 848 lbm/ft³, air density at normal conditions = 0.076 lbm/ft³.
solution_steps: Step 1: Pressure difference (eye vs far) = (30-22)=8 in Hg. Convert to seawater head: h_sw = (ρ_Hg/ρ_sw)×Δh_Hg = (848/64)×(8/12) ft = 8.83 ft.
Step 2: For part (b), apply Bernoulli between A (wind region) and B (eye, VB=0): PA + (1/2)ρ_air VA² = PB.
Step 3: Convert 155 mph to ft/s: 155×1.4667 = 227.3 ft/s.
Step 4: Air density at eye: ρ_air = (22/30)×0.076 = 0.0557 lbm/ft³.
Step 5: Dynamic pressure head in seawater: h_dyn = (ρ_air VA²/2) / (ρ_sw g) = (0.0557×(227.3)²/2) / (64×32.2) = 0.70 ft.
Step 6: Total swell = 8.83 + 0.70 = 9.53 ft.
formula_used: ΔP = ρ_sw g h_sw ; PA + ½ρ_air V² = PB ; h_dyn = (½ρ_air V²)/(ρ_sw g)
final_answer: Eye swell = 8.83 ft, total at point 2 = 9.53 ft
trap: 🚌 Assuming incompressible air at high speeds, or using wind speed in mph without conversion.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Compressible Bernoulli (Isothermal and Isentropic)
problem: Derive the Bernoulli equation for an ideal gas undergoing (a) an isothermal process and (b) an isentropic process.
solution_steps: Step 1: Starting from ∫ dP/ρ + V²/2 + gz = constant.
Step 2: For isothermal, P/ρ = RT → ρ = P/(RT) → ∫ dP/ρ = RT ln P.
Step 3: For isentropic, P/ρ^k = C → ρ = C^(1/k) P^(1/k) → ∫ dP/ρ = (k/(k-1)) P/ρ.
Step 4: Substitute to get final equations.
formula_used: Isothermal: RT ln P + V²/2 + gz = constant ; Isentropic: (k/(k-1)) P/ρ + V²/2 + gz = constant
trap: 🚨 Using incompressible Bernoulli for gases at high Mach numbers.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Effect of Friction on Temperature and Head Loss
problem: Show that during steady and incompressible flow of a fluid in an adiabatic flow section (a) the temperature remains constant and there is no head loss when friction is ignored and (b) the temperature increases and some head loss occurs when frictional effects are considered.
solution_steps: Step 1: For adiabatic flow, q=0. Entropy change s2 - s1 = c_v ln(T2/T1).
Step 2: Reversible (frictionless) → s2 = s1 → T2 = T1. Mechanical energy loss = 0 → head loss = 0.
Step 3: Irreversible → s2 > s1 → T2 > T1. Mechanical energy loss = u2 - u1 - q = c_v (T2 - T1) > 0 → head loss = (P2-P1)/ρ + (V2²-V1²)/2 + g(z2-z1) - w_shaft = positive.
formula_used: Δs = c_v ln(T2/T1) ; e_mech,loss = u2 - u1 - q
final_answer: For frictionless: T constant, h_L=0; with friction: T increases, h_L > 0.
trap: 🚨 Thinking that temperature cannot rise without heat transfer (but friction generates heat).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pump Efficiency and Temperature Rise
problem: A pump is powered by a 15-kW electric motor with 90% efficiency. Water flow rate = 50 L/s. Inlet and outlet diameters same, elevation difference negligible. Inlet pressure = 100 kPa, outlet = 300 kPa (absolute). Determine (a) mechanical efficiency of the pump and (b) temperature rise of water due to mechanical inefficiency.
solution_steps: Step 1: Shaft power = η_motor × Ẇ_electric = 0.9×15 = 13.5 kW.
Step 2: Mechanical energy increase of fluid = ṁ (P2-P1)/ρ = (50 kg/s)×(200 kPa)/(1000 kg/m³) = 10 kW.
Step 3: Pump efficiency = 10/13.5 = 0.741 = 74.1%.
Step 4: Lost mechanical power = 13.5 - 10 = 3.5 kW = ṁ c ΔT → ΔT = 3500/(50×4180) = 0.0167°C.
formula_used: η_pump = (ṁ ΔP/ρ) / Ẇ_shaft ; ΔT = Ė_loss / (ṁ c)
trap: 🚨 Using volume flow rate instead of mass flow rate, or forgetting to convert units (kW to W).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydroelectric Power Generation
problem: In a hydroelectric power plant, 100 m³/s of water flows from an elevation of 120 m to a turbine. Total irreversible head loss in piping (excluding turbine) is 35 m. Overall turbine-generator efficiency is 80%. Estimate the electric power output.
solution_steps: Step 1: Mass flow rate = 1000×100 = 10^5 kg/s.
Step 2: Use energy equation: available head = z1 - z2 - h_L = 120 - 0 - 35 = 85 m.
Step 3: Turbine power = ṁ g H = 10^5 × 9.81 × 85 = 83.385 MW.
Step 4: Electric power = η_turbine-gen × turbine power = 0.8 × 83.385 = 66.7 MW.
formula_used: Ẇ_turbine = ṁ g H_net ; Ẇ_electric = η_overall × ṁ g H_net
trap: 🚨 Adding head loss instead of subtracting, or forgetting to multiply by g.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Fan Selection for Computer Cooling
problem: A computer case of dimensions 12 cm × 40 cm × 40 cm is half filled with components. A 5-cm diameter hole is available for a fan that replaces the air in the void spaces once every second. Fan-motor efficiency = 30%, air density = 1.20 kg/m³. Determine (a) the wattage of the fan-motor unit and (b) the pressure difference across the fan.
solution_steps: Step 1: Void volume = 0.5×(0.12×0.4×0.4) = 0.0096 m³. Volume flow rate = 0.0096 m³/s.
Step 2: Mass flow rate = ρ V̇ = 1.2×0.0096 = 0.01152 kg/s.
Step 3: Outlet area = π(0.05²)/4 = 0.0019635 m². Outlet velocity = V̇/A = 0.0096/0.0019635 = 4.89 m/s.
Step 4: Mechanical energy increase = ṁ (α V²/2) = 0.01152×1.1×(4.89²/2) = 0.152 W (α=1.1).
Step 5: Electrical power = 0.152 / 0.3 = 0.506 W.
Step 6: Pressure difference = ρ × (α V²/2) = 1.2×1.1×(4.89²/2) = 15.8 Pa.
formula_used: Ẇ_fan,u = ṁ α V²/2 ; ΔP = ρ α V²/2 ; Ẇ_elect = Ẇ_fan,u / η_fan-motor
trap: 🚨 Using diameter instead of radius, or ignoring kinetic energy correction factor.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Head and Power Loss in Water Pumping
problem: Water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir by a pump that provides 20 kW of useful mechanical power to the water. The free surface of the upper reservoir is 45 m higher. Flow rate = 0.03 m³/s. Determine the irreversible head loss of the system and the lost mechanical power.
solution_steps: Step 1: Mass flow rate = 1000×0.03 = 30 kg/s.
Step 2: Useful power = ṁ g Δz + Ė_loss → 20×10³ = 30×9.81×45 + Ė_loss.
formula_used: Ẇ_pump,u = ṁ g Δz + Ė_loss ; h_L = Ė_loss/(ṁ g)
trap: 🚌 Assuming no head loss, or mixing up pump head and loss.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Newton’s Second Law and Linear Momentum
desc: For a system of mass m, Newton’s second law states that the net force equals mass times acceleration, or the rate of change of linear momentum. In fluid mechanics, this is used to relate forces to changes in fluid momentum.
formula: F = ma = d(mV)/dt; for a control volume: ΣF = d/dt∫_CV ρV dV + ∫_CS ρV(V_r·n) dA
trap: 🚨 Students often forget that momentum is a vector and that the velocity used in the control surface term must be relative to the control surface (V_r) while the momentum itself is absolute (V).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Forces Acting on a Control Volume
desc: Total force consists of body forces (gravity, electromagnetic) acting throughout the volume, and surface forces (pressure, viscous, reaction) acting on the control surface. In many problems, only weight, pressure forces, and reaction forces are considered.
formula: ΣF = ΣF_body + ΣF_surface = ∫_CV ρg dV + ∫_CS (-P n + τ·n) dA + ΣF_other
trap: 🚀 Using gage pressure automatically accounts for atmospheric pressure, which cancels out on all sides, but one must be careful when the control surface cuts through solid objects where reaction forces appear.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Momentum-Flux Correction Factor (β)
desc: Because velocity is not uniform over a cross section, a correction factor β is introduced to relate the actual momentum flux to the product of mass flow rate and average velocity. For laminar pipe flow, β = 4/3; for turbulent flow, β ≈ 1.01–1.04.
formula: β = (1/A) ∫_A (V/V_avg)² dA; actual momentum flux = β ṁ V_avg
trap: 🚨 Neglecting β in laminar flow can lead to significant errors (up to 33% error in momentum flux). Even in turbulent flow, β should be included for accuracy, though it is often omitted in practice.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Steady Linear Momentum Equation
desc: For steady flow, the time derivative term vanishes, and the net force equals the net outflow of momentum. For one inlet and one outlet, ΣF = ṁ(β₂V₂ – β₁V₁). This is widely used to compute reaction forces on nozzles, bends, and supports.
formula: ΣF = Σ_out β ṁ V – Σ_in β ṁ V
trap: 🚨 Using absolute velocities incorrectly when the control volume is moving; the relative velocity must be used for mass flow rate, but the absolute velocity appears in the momentum term.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Angular Momentum Equation (Moment of Momentum)
desc: The net torque on a control volume equals the rate of change of angular momentum inside the CV plus the net outflow of angular momentum. For steady flow, ΣM = Σ_out r × ṁ V – Σ_in r × ṁ V. It is essential for analyzing turbomachinery.
formula: ΣM = d/dt ∫_CV (r × ρV) dV + ∫_CS (r × ρV)(V_r·n) dA; steady: ΣM = Σ_out r ṁ V_t – Σ_in r ṁ V_t
trap: 🚨 Taking moments about the wrong point or forgetting that the tangential component of velocity is the only contributor to torque about the axis of rotation.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Momentum-Flux Correction Factor for Laminar Pipe Flow
problem: Consider laminar flow through a very long straight section of round pipe. The velocity profile is parabolic: V = 2V_avg (1 – r²/R²). Calculate the momentum-flux correction factor through a cross section of the pipe.
solution_steps: Use definition β = (1/A) ∫_A (V/V_avg)² dA.
Substitute V = 2V_avg (1 – r²/R²) and dA = 2πr dr.
β = [4/(πR²)] ∫_0^R (1 – r²/R²)² 2πr dr.
Let y = 1 – r²/R² → dy = –2r dr/R², limits y=1 at r=0, y=0 at r=R.
trap: 🚨 Assuming β = 1 for laminar flow; the actual value is 1.33, which can significantly affect momentum calculations.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Force to Hold a Deflector Elbow
problem: A reducing elbow deflects water flow upward 30° while accelerating it. Water flow rate = 14 kg/s, inlet area = 113 cm², outlet area = 7 cm², elevation difference = 30 cm. Neglect weight. Determine (a) inlet gage pressure and (b) anchoring force.
solution_steps: Compute velocities: V₁ = ṁ/(ρA₁) = 1.24 m/s, V₂ = 20.0 m/s.
Apply Bernoulli between inlet and outlet (neglecting losses): P₁/ρg + V₁²/2g + z₁ = P₂/ρg + V₂²/2g + z₂, with P₂ = 0 (gage).
Solve: P₁ = ρg[(V₂² – V₁²)/(2g) + z₂] = 202.2 kPa gage.
Momentum equation in x: F_Rx + P₁A₁ = β ṁ (V₂ cos 30° – V₁).
Momentum equation in z: F_Rz = β ṁ V₂ sin 30°.
Substitute β = 1.03, ṁ = 14 kg/s, compute F_Rx = –2053 N, F_Rz = 144 N.
formula_used: P₁ = P₂ + ρg[(V₂² – V₁²)/(2g) + z₂]; ΣF = Σ_out β ṁ V – Σ_in β ṁ V
final_answer: P₁ = 202.2 kPa (gage); F_Rx = 2053 N (to left), F_Rz = 144 N (upward)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to account for the change in elevation in Bernoulli; using absolute instead of gage pressure; neglecting β in laminar-like profiles.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Water Jet Striking a Stationary Plate
problem: A water jet of speed 20 m/s and flow rate 10 kg/s strikes a stationary vertical plate normally and splatters in all directions. Determine the force needed to hold the plate.
solution_steps: Choose CV that includes plate and cuts through support and water jet.
Steady flow, β ≈ 1.
Momentum equation in x-direction: ΣF_x = ṁ(V₂,x – V₁,x).
Inlet: V₁,x = V₁ = 20 m/s; outlet: V₂,x = 0 (water leaves tangential to plate).
Force on plate from water = ṁ V₁ = 10 kg/s × 20 m/s = 200 N.
Thus holding force = 200 N opposite to jet.
trap: 🚨 Thinking the force is half or double; using velocity magnitude without considering direction; forgetting that the splattered water has no x-momentum.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Wind Turbine Power and Force
problem: A wind turbine with 30-ft diameter blade span has cut-in wind speed 7 mph, generating 0.4 kW. Density of air = 0.076 lbm/ft³. Determine efficiency and horizontal force on mast. Also, effect of doubling wind speed.
solution_steps: Convert V₁ = 7 mph = 10.27 ft/s.
Mass flow rate = ρ V₁ A = 0.076 × 10.27 × (π×30²/4) = 551.7 lbm/s.
Exit velocity from energy balance: V₂ = V₁ √(1 – η) = 10.27√(0.673) = 8.43 ft/s.
Momentum equation: F = ṁ (V₂ – V₁) = 551.7 × (8.43 – 10.27)/32.2 = –31.5 lbf (force on turbine).
Doubling wind: power ∝ V³ → 3.2 kW; force ∝ V² → 126 lbf.
formula_used: η = Ẇ_act / (½ ρ A V₁³); F = ṁ(V₂ – V₁)
final_answer: η = 32.7%, F = 31.5 lbf; at 14 mph, power = 3.2 kW, force = 126 lbf
trap: 🚨 Using V instead of V² in mass flow; forgetting to convert units consistently; assuming efficiency independent of wind speed when it varies.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Satellite Repositioning by Rocket
problem: A 5000-kg satellite traveling at constant velocity fires a rocket that discharges 100 kg of gases at 3000 m/s relative to satellite in 2 s. Determine acceleration, velocity change, and thrust.
solution_steps: Choose control volume moving with satellite.
No external forces → 0 = m_sat dV_sat/dt + ṁ_f V_f (with V_f opposite direction).
Mass flow rate ṁ_f = 100 kg / 2 s = 50 kg/s.
Acceleration: a = (ṁ_f V_f)/m_sat = (50×3000)/5000 = 30 m/s².
Velocity change: ΔV = a Δt = 30×2 = 60 m/s.
final_answer: a = 30 m/s², ΔV = 60 m/s, thrust = 150 kN
trap: 🚨 Using absolute velocity of gases instead of relative velocity; forgetting that the satellite’s mass decreases during firing.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Net Force on a Flange
problem: Water flows at 18.5 gal/min through a flanged faucet. Inner diameter = 0.780 in, pressure at flange = 13.0 psig. Total weight of faucet assembly plus water = 12.8 lbf. Calculate net force on the flange.
solution_steps: Control volume includes faucet; forces: P₁A₁ (right), weight (down), reaction forces F_Rx, F_Rz.
Momentum x: P₁A₁ – F_Rx = ṁ V₁ (outlet has no x-momentum).
Momentum z: F_Rz – W = ṁ V₂ (outlet z-velocity = V).
Net force on flange = –F_R (action-reaction): –7.20 i – 11.8 k lbf.
formula_used: ΣF = Σ_out β ṁ V – Σ_in β ṁ V
final_answer: F_flange = –7.20 i – 11.8 k lbf (faucet pulls right and down)
trap: 🚨 Using absolute instead of gage pressure; forgetting to convert units of area and pressure consistently; sign errors in force balance.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Bending Moment at Pipe Base
problem: Water flows through a 10-cm-diameter L-shaped pipe (2 m vertical, 1 m horizontal) at 3 m/s. Horizontal pipe mass = 12 kg/m. Determine bending moment at base and length of horizontal section to make moment zero.
solution_steps: Mass flow rate: ṁ = ρAV = 1000×(π×0.1²/4)×3 = 23.56 kg/s.
Weight of horizontal section: W = 12×1×9.81 = 118 N.
Moment about base (point A): M_A = r₁W – r₂ ṁ V₂.
r₁ = 0.5 m (half of horizontal length), r₂ = 2 m (vertical height).
formula_used: ΣM = Σ_out r ṁ V – Σ_in r ṁ V
trap: 🚨 Forgetting that the weight acts at the center of the horizontal pipe; using incorrect sign for momentum term; mixing SI and English units.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Power Generation from Sprinkler Turbine
problem: A four-arm sprinkler rotates at 300 rpm. Water enters at 20 L/s, nozzle diameter 1 cm, radius 0.6 m. Estimate electric power produced (neglect losses).
solution_steps: Flow per nozzle: Q_nozzle = 20/4 = 5 L/s = 0.005 m³/s.
Jet velocity relative to nozzle: V_jet = Q_nozzle / A_jet = 0.005 / (π×0.005²) = 63.66 m/s.
Tangential speed of nozzle: V_nozzle = rω = 0.6 × (300×2π/60) = 18.85 m/s.
Relative velocity to ground: V_r = V_jet – V_nozzle = 44.81 m/s (in direction opposite rotation).
Torque from one arm: T = r × ṁ_nozzle × V_r = 0.6 × (5×1) × 44.81 = 134.4 N·m.
Total torque = 4 × 134.4 = 537.6 N·m.
formula_used: T = Σ r ṁ V_t; Ẇ = T ω
trap: 🚨 Using absolute jet velocity instead of relative; forgetting to divide total flow among nozzles; mixing rpm and rad/s incorrectly.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Dimensions and Units
desc: A dimension is a measure of a physical quantity without numerical values (e.g., length, mass, time). A unit is a way to assign a number to that dimension (e.g., meter, kilogram, second). There are seven primary dimensions, including mass, length, time, and temperature.
formula: Primary dimensions: {m}, {L}, {t}, {T}, etc.
trap: 🚨 Confusing dimensions with units; using inconsistent unit systems can lead to numerical errors.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Dimensional Homogeneity
desc: Every additive term in an equation must have the same dimensions. This law is used to nondimensionalize equations and identify dimensionless groups.
formula: All terms in an equation must have the same primary dimensions.
trap: 🚨 Adding terms with different dimensions is a sign of an error; dimensional homogeneity is a necessary check.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Nondimensionalization and Normalization
desc: Nondimensionalization divides each term by a collection of variables and constants to produce dimensionless terms. Normalization further ensures terms are of order unity. This reduces the number of parameters and reveals key dimensionless groups.
formula: Example: z* = z / z0, t* = w0 t / z0 leads to d²z*/dt*² = 1/Fr², where Fr = w0/√(g z0).
trap: 🚨 Confusing nondimensionalization with normalization; normalizing requires scaling to order unity.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Froude Number
desc: A dimensionless parameter representing the ratio of inertial force to gravitational force. It is important in free‑surface flows and flows with gravity effects.
formula: Fr = V / √(g L)  (or sometimes Fr = V²/(gL))
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong definition; some texts define Fr as V²/(gL); check context.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Reynolds Number
desc: Ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces; it governs transition to turbulence and similarity in viscous flows.
formula: Re = ρ V L / μ = V L / ν
trap: 🚌 Using an inappropriate characteristic length or velocity; values change based on flow geometry.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Similarity (Geometric, Kinematic, Dynamic)
desc: Complete similarity between model and prototype requires geometric similarity (same shape), kinematic similarity (velocity proportional), and dynamic similarity (forces proportional).
formula: If all independent Π groups match (e.g., Re, Fr), then dependent Π groups match.
trap: 🚨 Assuming similarity when only geometric similarity is satisfied; dynamic similarity requires matching all relevant Π groups.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Method of Repeating Variables
desc: A step‑by‑step procedure to generate dimensionless Π groups. It involves listing parameters, primary dimensions, choosing repeating parameters, and forcing products to be dimensionless.
formula: k = n - j, where n = total parameters, j = number of primary dimensions, k = number of Π groups.
trap: 🚨 Choosing inappropriate repeating parameters can yield awkward or non‑standard Π groups; follow guidelines (e.g., pick a length, velocity, density).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Buckingham Pi Theorem
desc: If a physical process involves n variables and these variables contain j independent primary dimensions, then the process can be described by n − j dimensionless Π groups.
formula: k = n - j
trap: 🚨 Miscounting the number of independent dimensions; sometimes j must be reduced if parameters do not span all dimensions.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Darcy Friction Factor
desc: A dimensionless parameter used to characterize wall shear stress in pipe flow; it is a function of Reynolds number and relative roughness.
formula: f = 8 τ_w / (ρ V²)
trap: 🚨 Confusing Darcy friction factor with Fanning friction factor (f_Fanning = τ_w / (½ρV²) = f/4).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Mach Number
desc: Ratio of flow speed to the speed of sound; indicates compressibility effects. For Ma < 0.3, flow is typically considered incompressible.
formula: Ma = V / c, where c = √(k R T) for ideal gas.
trap: 🚌 Using incompressible Bernoulli for Ma > 0.3 without checking compressibility effects.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Incomplete Similarity
desc: When it is impossible to match all independent Π groups between model and prototype, we accept incomplete similarity. Results can often be extrapolated if the dominant Π group is matched or if independence is achieved (e.g., Re‑independent drag).
formula: No single formula; typical approach: match Froude number in free‑surface flows, or match Reynolds number in pipe flows, and accept mismatch in others.
trap: 🚨 Extrapolating beyond validated range without verifying independence or using correction factors.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Primary Dimensions of Surface Tension
problem: An engineer is studying how some insects are able to walk on water. A fluid property of importance in this problem is surface tension (σ_s), which has dimensions of force per unit length. Write the dimensions of surface tension in terms of primary dimensions.
solution_steps: Step 1: Force has dimensions {m L / t²}.
Step 2: Surface tension = Force / Length → {σ_s} = {m L / t²} / {L} = {m / t²}.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting that force is mass × acceleration; including length incorrectly.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Dimensional Homogeneity of Bernoulli Equation
problem: Verify that each additive term in the Bernoulli equation P + ½ ρ V² + ρ g z = C has the same dimensions. What are the dimensions of the constant C?
solution_steps: Step 1: Write dimensions of each term: {P} = {m/(L t²)} (pressure = force/area).
Step 2: {½ ρ V²} = { (m/L³) (L²/t²) } = {m/(L t²)}.
Step 3: {ρ g z} = { (m/L³) (L/t²) L } = {m/(L t²)}.
Step 4: All terms have same dimensions, so C must have dimensions {m/(L t²)}.
trap: 🚨 Thinking pressure is force/volume; it's force/area.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Advantages of Nondimensionalization
problem: Your little brother’s high school physics class conducts experiments in a large vertical pipe under vacuum. They release a steel ball at initial height z0 between 0 and 15 m, with initial vertical speed w0 between 0 and 10 m/s. They hold w0 fixed at 4 m/s and vary z0, then hold z0 fixed at 10 m and vary w0. Prepare a nondimensional plot to show that all data collapse onto one family.
solution_steps: Step 1: Nondimensionalize: z* = z / z0, t* = w0 t / z0.
Step 2: The equation of motion gives d²z*/dt*² = -1/Fr² with Fr = w0/√(g z0).
Step 3: All trajectories in terms of z* and t* depend only on Fr².
Step 4: Replot the 10 original trajectories as z* vs t*; they fall on curves parameterized by Fr².
formula_used: z* = z/z0, t* = w0 t/z0, Fr = w0/√(g z0)
final_answer: The nondimensional plot shows that all trajectories collapse onto a single family; only Fr² matters.
trap: 🚌 Using wrong scaling parameters or forgetting to nondimensionalize both variables.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Extrapolation Using Nondimensional Data
problem: The gravitational constant on the moon is 1.63 m/s². An astronaut on the moon throws a baseball at 21.0 m/s at 5° above horizontal, from 2.0 m above the surface. Using the dimensionless data from Example 7‑3, predict how long it takes for the baseball to fall to the ground.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute vertical component w0 = 21.0 sin(5°) = 1.830 m/s.
Step 2: Froude number squared: Fr² = w0²/(g_moon z0) = (1.830)²/(1.63×2.0) ≈ 1.03.
Step 3: From Fig. 7‑13, for Fr² ≈ 1.03, t* ≈ 2.75 when z* = 0.
Step 4: Dimensional time t = t* z0 / w0 = 2.75 × 2.0 / 1.830 ≈ 3.01 s.
trap: 🚨 Using horizontal speed instead of vertical component, or mismatching scaling parameters.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Similarity between Model and Prototype Cars
problem: A one‑fifth scale model of a sports car is tested in a wind tunnel to predict drag at 50.0 mi/h at 25°C. The wind tunnel air is at 5°C. Determine the required wind tunnel speed for similarity.
solution_steps: Step 1: The dominant Π group is Reynolds number: Re = ρ V L / μ.
Step 2: Match Re between model and prototype: ρ_m V_m L_m / μ_m = ρ_p V_p L_p / μ_p.
Step 3: Solve for V_m = V_p (ρ_p/ρ_m) (L_p/L_m) (μ_m/μ_p).
Step 4: Use air properties: at 25°C, ρ = 1.184 kg/m³, μ = 1.849×10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s); at 5°C, ρ = 1.269 kg/m³, μ = 1.754×10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s).
trap: 🚨 Using length ratio incorrectly (L_p/L_m = 5, not 1/5).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Prediction of Aerodynamic Drag Force
problem: Using the wind tunnel test in Example 7‑5 at 221 mi/h, the measured drag force on the model car is 21.2 lbf. Predict the drag force on the prototype car at 50 mi/h and 25°C.
solution_steps: Step 1: Dynamic similarity ensures that the drag coefficient (Π₁) matches: CD = FD / (½ ρ V² A).
Step 2: Since Re matches, CD,m = CD,p → FD,p = FD,m (ρ_p/ρ_m) (V_p/V_m)² (A_p/A_m).
Step 3: Area ratio = (L_p/L_m)² = 5² = 25.
trap: 🚌 Forgetting to square the length ratio for area, or using wrong density ratio.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pressure in a Soap Bubble
problem: Establish a relationship between pressure difference ΔP = P_inside − P_outside, soap bubble radius R, and surface tension σ_s.
solution_steps: Step 1: List parameters: ΔP, R, σ_s. n = 3.
Step 2: Primary dimensions: {ΔP} = {m/(L t²)}, {R} = {L}, {σ_s} = {m/t²}.

Step 3: j = 2 (m and t; only two independent dimensions? Actually m, L, t → 3 dimensions, but we try j=2 after finding k=0; better: choose j=2 because m and t appear, but length is also there. Actually proper reduction: j = 2? Let's follow the text: they set j=3 first, got k=0, then reduced j=2, k=1.)
Step 4: Choose R and σ_s as repeating parameters.
Step 5: Form Π₁ = ΔP R^a σ_s^b → solve a = 1, b = -1 → Π₁ = ΔP R / σ_s.
Step 6: Since k=1, Π₁ = constant → ΔP = constant × σ_s / R.
final_answer: ΔP ∝ σ_s / R (constant = 4 from detailed analysis)
trap: 🚨 Not realizing that the constant cannot be determined by dimensional analysis alone.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Lift on a Wing (Similarity with Mach and Re)
problem: A wing with chord L_c = 1.12 m, area A = 10.7 m² is to fly at V = 52.0 m/s at 25°C. A one‑tenth scale model is to be tested in a pressurized wind tunnel (max 5 atm). Determine the required wind tunnel speed and pressure to achieve dynamic similarity, considering Mach number and Reynolds number.
solution_steps: Step 1: Dimensional analysis yields lift coefficient C_L = f(Re, Ma, α).
Step 2: For prototype, Ma_p = 52.0 / 346 ≈ 0.150 (subsonic).
Step 3: To match both Re and Ma, need V_m = V_p (L_p/L_m) (μ_m/μ_p)(ρ_p/ρ_m) for Re, and V_m = V_p (c_m/c_p) for Ma.
Step 4: At same temperature, c_m = c_p, so Ma matching gives V_m = V_p (L_p/L_m) = 520 m/s, which is supersonic and exceeds tunnel capability.

Step 5: For Ma < 0.3, compressibility negligible; match Re by pressurizing tunnel to increase density. With P_max = 5 atm, ρ_m ≈ 5ρ_p, μ changes little; V_m = V_p (L_p/L_m)(μ_m/μ_p)(ρ_p/ρ_m) = 52 × 10 × 1 × (1/5) = 104 m/s → Ma_m = 104/346 = 0.301, acceptable.
Step 6: Thus run tunnel at ~104 m/s and 5 atm.
formula_used: Re matching: V_m = V_p (L_p/L_m)(ρ_p/ρ_m)(μ_m/μ_p); Ma matching: V_m = V_p (c_m/c_p).
final_answer: Wind tunnel speed ≈ 104 m/s, pressure ≈ 5 atm, temperature 25°C.
trap: 🚨 Ignoring Mach number when prototype speed is low but model speed becomes high; failing to pressurize to increase density.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Friction in a Pipe (Darcy Friction Factor)
problem: Develop a nondimensional relationship between wall shear stress τ_w and other parameters for fully developed incompressible pipe flow: τ_w = f(V, ε, ρ, μ, D), where ε is average roughness height.
solution_steps: Step 1: List parameters: τ_w, V, ε, ρ, μ, D → n = 6.
Step 2: Primary dimensions: {τ_w} = {m/(L t²)}, {V} = {L/t}, {ε} = {L}, {ρ} = {m/L³}, {μ} = {m/(L t)}, {D} = {L}.
Step 3: j = 3 (m, L, t) → k = 3.
Step 4: Choose repeating parameters: V, D, ρ.
Step 5: Construct Π₁ = τ_w V^a D^b ρ^c → solve a = -2, b = 0, c = -1 → Π₁ = τ_w/(ρ V²).
Step 6: Π₂ = μ V^a D^b ρ^c → solve a = -1, b = -1, c = -1 → Π₂ = μ/(ρ V D) = 1/Re.
Step 7: Π₃ = ε V^a D^b ρ^c → solve a = 0, b = -1, c = 0 → Π₃ = ε/D.
Step 8: Manipulate: f = 8 τ_w/(ρ V²) = f(Re, ε/D).
trap: 🚨 Confusing Darcy friction factor with Fanning friction factor; using wrong exponent in Π₂.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Model Truck Wind Tunnel Measurements
problem: A 1/16th scale model truck is tested in a wind tunnel. Data: V (m/s) and FD (N) are given in Table 7‑7. The model width W = 0.159 m, height = 0.257 m. Plot CD vs Re, where CD = FD/(½ρ V² A_frontal) and Re = ρ V W / μ. Air at 25°C, ρ = 1.184 kg/m³, μ = 1.849×10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s). Estimate drag on the prototype traveling at 26.8 m/s.
solution_steps: Step 1: For each data point, compute Re = ρ V W / μ and CD = 2 FD/(ρ V² (W×height)).
Step 2: Plot CD vs Re; the curve levels off at Re ≈ 5×10⁵ to CD ≈ 0.76.
Step 4: Since CD is constant at high Re, use CD_p = 0.76.
formula_used: CD = 2 FD/(ρ V² A), Re = ρ V W/μ
trap: 🚨 Using wrong length scale for Re (e.g., using height instead of width), or forgetting to scale area correctly.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Model Lock and River (Incomplete Similarity)
problem: A 1:100 scale model of a river is to be built. What liquid would be appropriate to achieve complete similarity if both Froude number and Reynolds number must match?
solution_steps: Step 1: Match Fr: V_m/√(g L_m) = V_p/√(g L_p) → V_m/V_p = √(L_m/L_p) = √(1/100) = 0.1.
Step 2: Match Re: (V_m L_m)/ν_m = (V_p L_p)/ν_p → ν_m = ν_p (V_m/V_p)(L_m/L_p) = ν_p × 0.1 × 0.01 = ν_p × 0.001.
Step 3: For water at 20°C, ν_p ≈ 1.002×10⁻⁶ m²/s, then ν_m = 1.002×10⁻⁹ m²/s.
Step 4: No common liquid has such low kinematic viscosity; complete similarity impossible.
formula_used: Fr matching: V_m/V_p = √(L_m/L_p); Re matching: ν_m/ν_p = (V_m/V_p)(L_m/L_p)
final_answer: No suitable liquid; complete similarity impossible; must accept incomplete similarity.
trap: 🚨 Thinking water works for both; actual required viscosity is far too low.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Reynolds Number for Pipe Flow
desc: Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces, expressed as Re = ρVD/μ = VD/ν. For flow in a circular pipe, Re < 2300 indicates laminar flow, Re > 4000 indicates turbulent flow, and 2300 < Re < 4000 is transitional.
formula: Re = (ρ V D)/μ = (V D)/ν
trap: 🚨 Students often use the wrong characteristic length for non‑circular ducts; the hydraulic diameter Dh = 4Ac/p must be used instead of the nominal dimension.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Hydrodynamic Entrance Length
desc: The entrance region is where the velocity boundary layer develops until it fills the pipe. The hydrodynamic entry length Lh is the distance from the inlet to where the flow becomes fully developed. For laminar flow Lh ≈ 0.05 Re D; for turbulent flow Lh ≈ 10 D.
formula: L_h,lam = 0.05 Re D; L_h,turb ≈ 10 D
trap: 🚨 Assuming the entrance region is negligible for short pipes; entrance effects increase friction factor and pressure drop, especially in laminar flow.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Fully Developed Laminar Flow Velocity Profile
desc: In fully developed laminar pipe flow, the velocity profile is parabolic: u(r) = 2V_avg (1 – r²/R²). Maximum velocity is at the centerline, umax = 2V_avg. The average velocity is half the maximum.
formula: u(r) = 2 V_avg (1 – r²/R²); u_max = 2 V_avg; V_avg = (ΔP D²)/(32 μ L)
trap: 🚀 Using the laminar profile for turbulent flow; turbulent profiles are much flatter. Also forgetting that V_avg is half u_max only in laminar flow.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Darcy Friction Factor and Pressure Drop
desc: The pressure drop (head loss) due to friction in a pipe is given by ΔP = f (L/D) (ρV²/2), where f is the Darcy friction factor. For laminar flow f = 64/Re, independent of roughness. For turbulent flow f depends on Re and relative roughness e/D.
formula: ΔP = f (L/D) (ρ V²/2); h_L = f (L/D) (V²/(2g)); f = 64/Re (laminar)
trap: 🚨 Confusing the Darcy friction factor with the Fanning friction factor (which is f/4). Always check which definition is being used in formulas and charts.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Moody Chart and Colebrook Equation
desc: The Moody chart is a graphical representation of the Darcy friction factor f as a function of Reynolds number Re and relative roughness e/D. For turbulent flow, the Colebrook equation 1/√f = –2.0 log[(e/D)/3.7 + 2.51/(Re√f)] is used.
trap: 🚨 Using the Moody chart without verifying that the flow is indeed in the turbulent regime; the chart includes laminar and transition zones, so one must check Re first.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Minor Losses in Pipe Systems
desc: Minor losses are caused by fittings, valves, bends, expansions, contractions, and other components. They are expressed as h_L = K_L V²/(2g), where K_L is the loss coefficient. Minor losses may be significant in short pipes with many components.
formula: h_L,minor = K_L (V²/(2g))
trap: 🚨 Neglecting minor losses when they are comparable to major losses; always check the total head loss. Also using the wrong velocity (e.g., using large pipe velocity for a contraction).

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Equivalent Length Method
desc: Minor losses can be expressed as an equivalent length of straight pipe that would cause the same head loss: L_eq = (K_L D)/f. The total head loss is then computed using the total length (actual + equivalent) in the Darcy–Weisbach equation.
formula: L_eq = (K_L D)/f; h_L = f (L + ΣL_eq)/D · V²/(2g)
trap: 🚀 Using the same friction factor f for the equivalent length as for the straight pipe; f depends on Re and roughness, so the equivalent length is not constant with flow rate.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Pipes in Series and Parallel
desc: For pipes in series, the flow rate is the same through each pipe, and total head loss is the sum of individual head losses. For pipes in parallel, the head loss is the same across each branch, and total flow rate is the sum of individual flow rates.
formula: Series: Q = Q₁ = Q₂; h_L,total = h_L1 + h_L2; Parallel: h_L1 = h_L2; Q_total = Q₁ + Q₂
