name: Stator Vane Design for Axial Fan
problem: Design stator vanes for a vane-axial fan with D=1 m, axial speed 50 m/s, chord 20 cm, trailing edge angle 63°. Determine minimum number of vanes to achieve at least 45° outlet flow angle without separation.
solution_steps: Step 1: Approximate 3D stator as 2D cascade at average radius, vary blade spacing s (10–60 cm).
Step 2: 2D steady RANS (k-ε) with periodic BCs, structured grid near vanes.
Step 3: Compute outlet flow angle b_avg and check for separation (vorticity plots).
Step 4: s=30 cm gave b_avg ≈ 49.7°, no separation; s=40 cm gave b_avg ≈ 43.2°, separation visible.
Step 5: Max number of vanes N = πD / s ≈ 10.5 at tip → use 10 or 11 vanes.
Step 6: Full 3D simulation (10 vanes) confirmed b_avg ≈ 53.3°, mild separation near tip, acceptable.
formula_used: Blade spacing s = (πD)/N; 2D cascade model; k-ε turbulence model
final_answer: Minimum 10 vanes required; with 10 vanes outlet flow angle ≈ 53°, slight separation near tip acceptable.
trap: 🚨 Using 2D cascade approximation at average radius may not capture radial variations; 3D simulation confirmed mild tip separation, which might need twist correction.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Cross-Flow Heat Exchanger with Rough Tubes
problem: Model turbulent air flow (Re ≈ 1×10⁵) through a staggered tube bank; tubes at 500 K, inlet air 300 K. Compare heat transfer for a=0°, a=10°, and effect of surface roughness (e/D=0.01).
solution_steps: Step 1: 2D periodic domain with translationally periodic BCs; structured grid fine near tubes.
Step 2: k-ε turbulence model; smooth tubes; a=0° gave ΔT_avg = 5.51 K; a=10° gave 5.65 K (2.5% increase).
Step 3: Increasing inlet turbulence intensity to 25% at a=0° gave ΔT_avg = 5.87 K (6.5% increase).
Step 4: Smooth tubes replaced with rough tubes (e/D=0.01, wall functions); ΔT_avg = 14.48 K (163% increase).
formula_used: Roughness model in k-ε; wall heat flux specified
final_answer: Off-axis inlet flow slightly improves heat transfer; roughness dramatically increases heat transfer (163% rise).
trap: 🚌 Roughness must be physically modeled with appropriate wall treatment; if cells are too fine, roughness model becomes invalid.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Cooling of Integrated Circuit Chips
problem: PCB with 8 ICs (each dissipating 6.24 W) in either long or short configuration. Cooling air at 30°C, 2.60 m/s through 2 cm gap. Determine which configuration gives lower max chip temperature and if 180°C limit is met.
solution_steps: Step 1: 3D structured grid (267,520 cells) for each configuration; k-ε turbulence model.
Step 2: Boundary conditions: velocity inlet (2.60 m/s, 30°C), pressure outlet, adiabatic walls except chip surfaces (heat flux: top 4800 W/m², sides 1200 W/m²).
Step 3: Long config: T_max = 187.5°C (chip 7), avg top = 144.5°C. Short config: T_max = 182.1°C (chips 7,8), avg top = 144.7°C.
Step 4: Both exceed 180°C limit; short config slightly better but still insufficient.
formula_used: Convective heat transfer; k-ε turbulence model with wall functions
final_answer: Short configuration yields lower max temperature (182.1°C vs 187.5°C), but both exceed 180°C limit.
trap: 🚨 Low Reynolds number turbulent flows (Re ≈ 3600) are challenging for standard turbulence models; comparisons between configurations are more reliable than absolute temperatures.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Compressible Flow in Converging–Diverging Nozzle
problem: Simulate inviscid compressible flow of air through an axisymmetric C–D nozzle. Inlet stagnation pressure 220 kPa, static pressure 210 kPa, T0=300 K. Vary back pressure to observe normal shocks and compare to 1D theory.
solution_steps: Step 1: 2D axisymmetric domain with structured grid (12,000 cells).
Step 2: Pressure inlet (P0, P, T0), pressure outlet (Pb).
Step 3: For Pb=50 kPa (Pb/P0=0.227), supersonic throughout diverging section; Mach number and pressure match 1D isentropic theory well.
Step 4: For Pb=100, 150, 200 kPa, normal shock appears in diverging section; shock location moves upstream with increasing Pb.
Step 5: Shock shape is curved; 1D theory using normal shock relations agrees closely with averaged CFD results.
formula_used: Isentropic relations; normal shock relations; 1D compressible flow
final_answer: CFD matches 1D theory; normal shocks form as predicted; mass flow choked at throat.
trap: 🚌 Assuming a straight shock; actual shocks in axisymmetric nozzles are curved. Adding viscosity and turbulence moves shock upstream due to boundary layer displacement effect.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Oblique Shocks over a Wedge
problem: Compute inviscid 2D compressible flow (Ma=2.0) over wedges with half-angles 10°, 20°, and 30°. Compare shock angles and downstream Mach numbers to oblique shock theory.
solution_steps: Step 1: 2D domain with pressure far field BC (Ma=2, static pressure, T), wedge wall, symmetry plane.
Step 2: For 10° wedge: attached oblique shock, shock angle ≈ 40° (theory 39.31°), downstream Ma=1.64.
Step 3: For 20° wedge: attached oblique shock, shock angle ≈ 54° (theory 53.4°), downstream Ma=1.21.
Step 4: For 30° wedge: shock detached (bow wave) because wedge angle exceeds maximum deflection for attached shock at Ma=2 (≈23°).
final_answer: Excellent agreement with oblique shock theory for attached shocks; detached bow wave forms for 30° wedge.
trap: 🚌 Forgetting that attached oblique shocks exist only up to a maximum wedge angle; beyond that, a detached bow wave forms.

subject: Fluid Mechanics
name: Open-Channel Flow over a Bump
problem: Simulate 2D open-channel flow (water/air) over a 0.1‑m‑high bump. Inlet conditions: (a) subcritical (y=0.3 m, V=0.5 m/s); (b) supercritical (y=0.5 m, V=4.0 m/s); (c) subcritical to supercritical (y=0.5 m, V=1.0 m/s). Predict free surface shape.
solution_steps: Step 1: 2D domain with two-phase (air and water) inviscid flow; velocity inlet split (water and air), pressure outlet, wall bottom, top inviscid wall.
Step 2: Case (a) Fr=0.291 (subcritical): free surface dips over bump, remains subcritical downstream.
Step 3: Case (b) Fr=1.81 (supercritical): free surface rises over bump, remains supercritical.
Step 4: Case (c) Fr_inlet=0.452 (subcritical) → after bump depth decreases, velocity increases, becomes supercritical (Fr=1.28).
formula_used: Froude number Fr = V/√(gy); two-phase volume-of-fluid (VOF) method
final_answer: CFD correctly predicts free surface behavior: subcritical flow dips, supercritical rises; transition from subcritical to supercritical occurs downstream of bump.
trap: 🚌 Assuming the free surface remains horizontal; the bump causes local acceleration/deceleration that changes depth.
